coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and chemical modification of carbon nanodots/monolayer hexagonal boron nitride/substrate heterostructures and their terahertz optoelectronic properties
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Hua
Wang, Boyang
Cheng, Xingjia
Song, Dan
Xiao, Huan
Xu, Wen
Lu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) can be potentially applied in optoelectronic devices such as full-color display and LED. In this study we fabricate, for the first time, CNDs/monolayer hexagonal boron nitride (ML-hBN)/sapphire heterostructures, where i) ML-hBN is compatible with both CNDs and sapphire due to the presence of the van der Waals (vdW) forces between ML-hBN and these materials and ii) the CNDs can be chemically modified by attaching chemical bonds and/or functional groups on the surface/edges of the carbon-cores of CNDs. The basic physical and chemical properties of CNDs and the heterostructures are characterized. We further apply terahertz time-domain spectroscopy to measure the optical conductivity of CNDs/ML-hBN vdW heterostructures and to determine optically the key physical parameters of these heterostructures. The temperature dependence of these parameters is examined. We find that the agglomeration of dried CNDs on sapphire substrate can be largely suppressed by the presence of ML-hBN and the electronic and optoelectronic properties of the heterostructure can be controlled and modulated by chemical modification on CNDs. The results obtained from this study can be helpful for the fabrication, integration and modulation of CND based vdW heterostructures for practical device applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams of THz TDS transmission measurement system (left panel) and of the CNDs/ML-hBN/sapphire sample structure (right panel). The inset on the upper right shows the CNDs with attached chemical functional groups.

(a-c) HRTEM images of GCNDs, BGCNDs and YCNDs samples, respectively. The upper insets show the SEM images of the cross-sections of CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures, where the thickness of the CND layer is indicated; the middle ones show the lattice fringes of the isolated CNDs, where the corresponding lattice spacing is indicated; and the lower insets show the statistical histogram of the size distributions of CNDs, where the average size of CNDs is indicated. (d) the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CNDs in different solutions. (e)-(g) the PL spectra of GCNDs, BGCNDs and YCNDs in different solutions measured by different excitation wavelengths as indicated.

Surface micrographs of (a) GCNDs on sapphire substrate and (b)-(d) GCNDs/, BGCNDs/ and YCNDs/ML-hBN/sapphire samples, observed by optical microscopy. These photos are taken under the same lumen of the light.

(a)-(d) Full-scan XPS spectra of ML-hBN and GCNBs/, BGCNBs/ and YCNBs/ML-hBN/sapphire heterostructures, respectively. The percentages of atomic contents are indicated. The corresponding high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s in (e)-(h), O1s in (i)-(l), and N1s in (m)-(p). The deconvolutions of the high resolution XPS spectra are presented accordingly.

Spectra of THz transmittance for (a) ML-hBN and (b) GCNDs/ and (c) BGCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure at different temperatures as indicated. Real σ 1(ω) and imaginary σ 2(ω) parts of optical conductivity in (d) ML-hBN and (e) GCNDs/ and (f) BGCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure as a function of radiation frequency f = ω/2π at different temperatures. In (d)-(f), the dots are experimental results and the solid curves are given by the Drude-Smith formula.

(a) Correlation parameter of carrier density N, (b) electronic relaxation time τ and (c) electronic localization factor c as a function of temperature for different samples as indicated. The dots are experimental results obtained via fitting with the Drude-Smith formula. In (a), the solid curves are obtained by fitting with Eq. (3) and the bottom panel for ML-hBN is with N = n/m*. In (b), the black straight line for ML-hBN is drawn to guide the eye and the other curves are obtained by fitting with Eq. (4). In (c), the straight lines are drawn to guide the eye.

Peak positions and percentages of C1s, O1s, and N1s obtained from full-scan XPS spectra of ML-hBN and GCNBs/, BGCNBs/ and YCNBs/ML-hBN/sapphire heterostructures. And the peak positions of chemical bonds/functional groups and their area percentages obtained from the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, and N1s after deconvolution.
Parameters obtained from fitting of Eq. (4) to electronic relaxation times obtained experimentally for CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures.

head.ce:title: Fabrication and chemical modification of carbon nanodots/monolayer hexagonal boron nitride/substrate heterostructures and their terahertz optoelectronic properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) can be potentially applied in optoelectronic devices such as full-color display and LED. In this study we fabricate, for the first time, CNDs/monolayer hexagonal boron nitride (ML-hBN)/sapphire heterostructures, where i) ML-hBN is compatible with both CNDs and sapphire due to the presence of the van der Waals (vdW) forces between ML-hBN and these materials and ii) the CNDs can be chemically modified by attaching chemical bonds and/or functional groups on the surface/edges of the carbon-cores of CNDs. The basic physical and chemical properties of CNDs and the heterostructures are characterized. We further apply terahertz time-domain spectroscopy to measure the optical conductivity of CNDs/ML-hBN vdW heterostructures and to determine optically the key physical parameters of these heterostructures. The temperature dependence of these parameters is examined. We find that the agglomeration of dried CNDs on sapphire substrate can be largely suppressed by the presence of ML-hBN and the electronic and optoelectronic properties of the heterostructure can be controlled and modulated by chemical modification on CNDs. The results obtained from this study can be helpful for the fabrication, integration and modulation of CND based vdW heterostructures for practical device applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are a class of zero-dimensional surface-passivated materials with typical sizes of several nanometers [1]. The most important feature of CNDs is that the chemical bonds/functional groups can be easily attached or dopped to the surface/edges of the carbon cores of the CNDs [2–4] with mainly graphite-like material structures [5–7]. Thus, the physical properties of CNDs can be modulated efficiently by, e.g., chemical doping and/or surface modification [8]. The CNDs have exhibited excellent optical and optoelectronic properties, such as tunable photoluminescence (PL) from deep ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR) [9,10] with high fluorescent quantum yield [2], ultrafast injection and extraction of conducting carriers [11], terahertz (THz) photo-induced insulator-to-semiconductor transition [12], modulation of the sonoluminescence effect [9], photocatalytic [13], etc., to mention but a few. These outstanding features make the CNDs available for wide applications, especially in the field of optoelectronic materials and devices. In particular, light-emitting diode (LED) and display are two of the most important device applications of the fluorescent CNDs (FCNDs), which can be utilized for the fabrication and realization of full-color lighting and displays [14–17]. Similar to semiconductor-based electronic and optoelectronic devices, the successful exploitation of the electronic and optoelectronic properties of CNDs relies largely on appropriate integration of CNDs with the substrate [18]. In general, depositing or dispersing CNDs directly on a substrate often leads to the agglomeration of dried CNDs on the substrate, which can result in some negative effects such as the decrease in optical transmittance due to Rayleigh scattering, the fluorescent quenching, the phase separation [18,19], etc. Therefore, to disperse/deposit the CNDs uniformly on the dielectric substrate and to integrate CNDs with the solid-state matrices, while maintaining the excellent physical properties of CNDs, have been the important and vital issues for the application of the CNDs.

At present, mesoporous [20,21] and two-dimensional (2D) layered materials [22,23] have been applied as substrates to host solid-state or dry CND particles in order to fabricate the electronic and optical devices based on CNDs. It is known that hexagonal boron nitride (hBN), also referred as white graphite, is a close analogue of graphite with a similar honeycomb structure, where each pair of carbon atoms in the unit cell of graphite is replaced by boron and nitrogen. Similar to graphite, bulk hBN crystals can also be exfoliated into few or even mono atomic layers (ML). ML-hBN has been widely explored as ultra-flat insulating substrates [24], atomically thin tunnelling barriers [25–27], encapsulation materials [28,29], defect-free dielectrics [29,30], etc. Currently, graphene and carbon nanotubes have been applied for realizing C-based 2D-2D [31] and 1D-2D [32] van der Waals (vdW) heterostructures (vdWHs) on layered 2D hBN. In contrast to semiconductor-based heterostructures [33], the vdWHs can be formed via relatively weak vdW coupling between two material layers. Thus, the formation of heterostructure does not necessarily require the coordination of the chemical bonds and/or the match of the lattice structures between two different materials. This kind of hybridized structure can effectively achieve the complementary function of two materials and can enhance the inter-layer charge transfer and reduce the interface resistance [34,35]. It should be noted that ML-hBN is compatible with both CNDs and a substrate such as sapphire owing to the presence of the vdW forces between ML-hBN and these materials. Therefore, it is interesting and significant to fabricate the CNDs/ML-hBN/substrate heterostructures in which the agglomeration of dry CNDs on dielectric substrate can be greatly reduced. This becomes the prime motivation of the present study.

In this study, we first prepare CNDs which can be dissolved in different chemical solutions to obtain CNDs with chemical modification. The basic optical and chemical properties of CNDs with different chemical modifications in different solutions are examined. We then fabricate the CNDs/ML-hBN/substrate heterostructures in which CNDs with different chemical modifications are taken. The basic physical properties of these heterostructures are characterized by using different techniques. Moreover, we employ THz time-domain spectroscopy (TDS) to examine the electronic and optoelectronic properties of these heterostructures. The major advantage of THz TDS is that we can measure the key physical parameters of these heterostructures without the need of fabricating the contact electrodes on the sample. It should be noted that the THz photon energy is much smaller than the LUMO-HOMO energy gap (normally in UV regime) and the intermediate energy spacings (normally in visible regime) in CNDs and in ML-hBN. Thus, THz irradiation does not produce photon-induced carriers and the associated exciton effects [36] in CNDs and in ML-hBN. THz TDS can be used to study the dynamics properties of free carrier in CNDs/ML-hBN vdWHs. Very recently, we have applied the THz TDS technique for the investigation of graphene [37], ML-hBN on different substrates [38,39] and of dry CND particles [12]. In the present study, we apply the similar experimental approaches for the investigation of CNDs/ML-hBN/substrate heterostructures. The central aim of this study is to examine the influence of chemical modification on CNDs on electronic and optoelectronic properties of CNDs/ML-hBN/substrate heterostructures.

In this study, the standard technique of the chemical vapor deposition (CVD) was used to grow ML-hBN on copper foil, followed by transferring of ML-hBN onto sapphire substrate using a standard sealed transfer method [40,41]. Briefly, i) a tube furnace with single temperature zone was applied for the preparation of the ML-hBN; ii) a copper foil was heated up to 1050 °C under the continuous pass of 10 sccm hydrogen and 100 sccm argon gases; iii) the copper foil was subsequently annealed at 1050 °C for 1 h; iv) borane ammonia complex (NH3·BH3) was used as nitrogen and boron sources for the synthesis of hBN; v) 0.02 g of borane ammonia complex was placed away from the high temperature zone and heated to 100 °C and held for 20 min to decompose to hydrogen, ammonia borane (NH2·BH2) and gaseous borazine ((HBNH)3); vi) the gaseous (HBNH)3 was oxidized to hBN on a copper foil at a temperature of 1050 °C; vii) the ML-hBN synthesized on the copper foil was coated with polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA). After the copper foil was etched off with FeCl3 solution, the ML-hBN was cleaned by deionized water and transferred onto a sapphire substrate; and viii) the PMMA coated on the hBN film was etched off with acetone. Thus, the ML-hBN on sapphire substrate can be obtained after the acetone evaporates. The area of ML-hBN used in this study is 10 × 10 mm2, which covers the entire sapphire substrate.

CNDs were synthesized by using o-phenylenediamine and Ninhydrin by applying the standard hydrothermal method. Specifically, 1 mmol o-phenylenediamine and 1 mmol Ninhydrin were added to 10 mL of water. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave after sonication and reaction at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtrated through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and finally dialyzed with the membrane (500 Da) for 48 h to remove the unreacted precursors. The solution in the dialysis bag is freeze-dried to obtain CND powder.

The key feature of CND powder synthesized in this study is that when dispersing them in different chemical solutions, different colors of PL can be generated. For example, when dispersing them in dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol and acetic acid respectively and shaking ultrasonically for 10 min, the green, bright green and yellow PL emission can be observed respectively. We name these CNDs respectively as GCNDs, BGCNDs and YCNDs.

The small amount of GCNDs, BGCNDs and YCNDs in the solutions (∼0.5 mL) were taken respectively by rubber tipped droppers and dropped onto the surface of ML-hBN film on sapphire substrate. Then the samples were kept in oven at 45 ℃ for 30–60 min to make the solvents fully evaporate. We note that this is also a low-temperature annealing process. In doing so, three kinds of CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures on sapphire substrate were obtained. The schematic diagram of the sample structure is shown in the right panel of Fig. 1 . We name them as GCNDs-hBN, BGCNDs-hBN and YCNDs-hBN respectively. Thanks to the unique feature of infiltration of hBN with chemical solutions [42], the dry CNDs can disperse rather uniformly on ML-hBN without significant agglomeration.

In this study, the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN, USA), the scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Sirion 200, USA), the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (TU-1810PC, China), the PL (FLS1000, UK), the optical micrograph (SM, AX10, Germany), and the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, UK) were applied for the characterization of the samples. All these measurements were conducted at room temperature.

We apply the technique of THz TDS for studying the basic optoelectronic properties. The schematic diagram of THz TDS transmission measurement system used in this study is shown in the left panel of Fig. 1. i) A femtosecond (fs) fiber laser (ROI optoelectronics, 1550 nm central wavelength, 80 fs pulse duration, 100 MHz repetition rate) is divided into the pump and probe beams respectively. ii) The pump beam is irradiated on an InGaAs photoconductance antenna (PCA, Menlno, Germany) to generate the pulsed THz radiation with a spectral range from 0.1 to 2.0 THz. The generated THz beam is highly linearly polarized. iii) The THz beam is focused and irradiated vertically on the surface of the sample (shown in the right panel in Fig. 1) placed in a cryostat (ST-500, Jains, UK). iv) The cryostat is sealed by quartz optical windows to ensure a constant temperature. Since the quartz absorbs high frequency THz waves, the effective THz frequency for the measurement is in the regime of 0.1–1.2 THz. The measurement temperature is altered from 80 K to 300 K using liquid nitrogen; v) The probe fs laser beam is irradiated on another InGaAs PCA after a time delay for the detection of the THz beam transmitted through the sample. vi) The experimental system is slowly flushed with dry nitrogen gas to prevent the absorption of the THz wave by the moisture and dust in free-space. And vii) during the measurement, the vacuum pressure inside the cryostat is maintained at about 10-7 mbar. Using this experimental setup, we can measure the transmission electric field strength through a sample as a function of delay time at different temperatures.

The HRTEM images of GCNDs, BGCNDs and YCNDs are shown in Fig. 2 (a)-(c), respectively. In the middle insets in Fig. 2(a)-(c), the lattice fringes of the carbon cores of the CNDs are evident. The average lattice spacing of three kinds of CNDs is about 0.21 nm, corresponding to crystallographic spacing of the (100)-facet of the graphite structure [43,44]. Therefore, the CNDs synthesized in this work have good C-based crystallographic structures. Because GCNDs, BGCNDs and YCNDs were obtained by dispersing the same CND powders in different chemical solutions, their C-cores are essentially the same. Different solvents can result in different kinds and numbers of chemical bonds/functional groups on the surface/edge of the C-cores and, thus, achieve different chemical modifications. The results from HRTEM images show that the size and morphology of the CNDs can be varied slightly by chemical modification. Furthermore, we used the Image J software to count about 120 CNDs to obtain the particle sizes of the CNDs (the lower insets in Fig. 2(a)-(c)). We obtain that the average sizes of these CNDs are respectively about 4.66 nm, 5.11 nm and 3.58 nm.

We apply the SEM to examine the uniformity of the CND layer on top of ML-hBN and to measure the thickness of the CND layer. The results are shown in the upper insets in Fig. 2(a)-(c) for different CNDs, where the SEM images of the cross-sections of CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures are presented. Because ML-hBN can infiltrate nicely with chemical solutions [42], CNDs can disperse rather uniformly on the surface of ML-hBN and no significant agglomeration of CNDs is observed. We find that the thicknesses of GCND, BGCND and YCND layers on ML-hBN are, respectively, 3.01, 8.43 and 5.11 μm. For sample preparation, the thickness of CND layer depends on the concentration of solution for dispersing CNDs, the dose of the drops onto ML-hBN, and on the temperature for solvent evaporating. It should be noted that because the thickness of ML-hBN is very thin, the ML-hBN sandwiched between CNDs and sapphire substrate cannot be clearly seen by SEM.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CNDs in different solutions are shown in Fig. 2(d). The characteristic absorption peaks can be observed in the UV region of 250–350 nm. They are attributed to the π → π* transition of C–C/C = C bands [45–47]. In addition, the characteristic absorption peaks of CNDs can also be seen in visible bandwidths in Fig. 2(d) in 350–500 nm regime. They come from electronic transitions associated in energy states induced by functional groups/bonds attached to the CNDs [48]. Moreover, the PL spectra from CNDs in different solutions are shown in Fig. 2(e)–(g), under the optimal excitation wavelengths. The strongest PL peaks of three types of CNDs are located at 534 nm, 542 nm and 572 nm respectively, corresponding to green, bright green and yellow colors. The above results indicate that the functional groups/bonds attached to the edge/surface of the C-cores of CNDs induce the surface or intermediate states in CNDs and can play an important role for optical absorption and PL from CNDs [49]. We note that in this study, the excitation and generation of PL occur among the surface states and HUMO states in CNDs. The absorption edges shown in Fig. 2(d) do not necessarily correspond to the PL peaks shown in Fig. 2(e)-(g). A detailed discussion on this point is presented in Supplementary material.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the optical micrograph of dried CNDs on sapphire substrate directly. It can be seen that CNDs agglomerate obviously on sapphire. It is known that the aggregation of CNDs may form the irregular graphite structures and, thus, the advantages of CNDs in electronics and optoelectronics may be deprived. Fig. 3(b)-(d) show the surface micrographs of three CNDs/ML-hBN/sapphire heterostructure samples under the same lumen of the light. Combined with the SEM images for these three heterostructures (see Fig. S1 in Supplementary material), we find that the distribution of CNDs in the CND layer is very homogeneous. There is no remarkable agglomeration of the carbon dots in the CND layer after solvent evaporation. This indicates that ML-hBN can play an important role in improving effectively the interfaces with CNDs and with sapphire substrate to avoid the agglomeration of dry CNDs. The presence of small bumps on the sample surfaces are due to slow evaporation of the solvents, resulting in slight high and low undulations of CNDs on the sample surfaces. For GCNDs and BGCNDs, the slight agglomeration of carbon dots can be seen (Fig. 3(b) and (c)) but the dimension of the agglomerated dots is less than 1 μm. In our THz TDS measurement, the diameter of the focused THz beam is about 3–5 mm and the wavelength of 1 THz is 300 μm. As a result, the slight agglomeration and fluctuation of CNDs on the sample surface do not affect markedly the results of THz TDS measurement. Furthermore, we note that the degree of the darkness in different samples shown in Fig. 3(c)-(d) corresponds to different thicknesses of the CND layers in heterostructures. An important and significant result which we obtain here is that the presence of ML-hBN in the heterostructure can largely suppress the agglomeration of CNDs on ML-hBN and, as a result, can improve greatly the quality of the heterostructure sample.

We utilize the XPS to further investigate CNDs/ML-hBN/sapphire heterostructures prepared in this study. The full-scan XPS spectra for ML-hBN and for CNDs on ML-hBN are shown in Fig. 4 (a)-(d), respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, and N1s for corresponding four samples and their deconvolution spectra are shown in Fig. 4(e)-(h), (i)-(l), and (m)-(p), respectively. Table 1 shows the peak positions and the percentages of C1s, O1s and N1s for the full-scan XPS spectra of four samples, as well as the peak positions of chemical bonds/functional groups and area percentages obtained from the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s and N1s after deconvolution. The scanning depth of the XPS used in the measurements is about 10 nm, so the XPS spectra of ML-hBN are affected mainly by sapphire substrate (Al2O3). Although the volatile ethanol and acetic acid cannot make a big difference in O-based functional groups in the CNDs [50], they can result in different PL peaks observed for BGCNDs and YCNDs. As can be seen from Table 1, the contents of C, O, and N in BGCNDs/ and YCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures are quite similar and their functional group classes and contents are also very close. This is because of less contents of functional groups left on CNDS due to the quick volatilization of ethanol and acetic acid solvents. We note that the DMF solvent can introduce more C-N and C=O bonds into the CNDs [51], which makes the ratio of O and N in GCNDs significantly higher than other two CNDs. According to Table 1, the contents of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N in GCNDs/ML-hBN sample are higher than those in other two CNDs. Compared to BGCNDs/ and YCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures, CNDs in GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure contain more C=O bonds and N-group functional groups. These differences in chemical modifications on CNDs not only produce a certain degree of alteration in the PL from CNDs, but also have a significant effect on the THz optoelectronic properties of CNDs/ML-hBN/sapphire heterostructures.

In this work, the samples of bare sapphire substrate, ML-hBN/sapphire and CNDs/ML-hBN/sapphire heterostructures were measured via THz TDS transmission experiments at different temperatures (80, 100, 120, 150, 180, 210, 240, 270, 300 K). First, we measure respectively the THz transmission electric fields in time-domain for bare sapphire substrate Esub (t) and samples Esam (t). The corresponding transmission electric fields in frequency-domain, Esub (ω) and Esam (ω), then can be obtained through the Fourier transformation of Esub (t) and Esam (t). Thus, the transmittance of CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure can be obtained by T(ω) = |E sam(ω)|2/|E sub(ω|2. The transmittance spectra for ML-hBN and GCNDs/, BGCNDs/ and YCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures are shown in Fig. 5 (a)-(c) and in Fig. S2 in Supplementary material, respectively. It can be seen that the presence of CNDs on ML-hBN can significantly reduce THz transmission and the reduction is frequency dependent. Since the types and contents of the chemical bonds/functional groups attached to the surface/edges of the C-cores are similar, the reductions of THz transmission by GCNDs and YCNDs on ML-hBN are also very similar. However, the transmittance of GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure decreases with increasing radiation frequency, indicating that the heterostructure formed by CNDs with more N-based functional groups and C=O bonds can strongly reduce high frequency THz transmission. Compared to BGCNDs/ (8.43 μm thick) and YCNDs/ML-hBN (5.11 μm thick) heterostructures, the THz transmittance of the thinner GCNDs/ML-hBN (3.01 μm thickness) heterostructure decreases more markedly. This is because the CNDs contain more N-based functional groups, which can provide more electrons undergoing the intra-band transitions and enhance the absorption of THz photons. Thus, the enhanced optical conductance of GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure can be expected.

In our experiments, the incident THz radiation field and the THz detector are linearly polarized along the same direction. As a result, Esam (ω) and Esub (ω) are longitudinal electric field strengths parallel to the polarization direction of the incident THz beam. Therefore, the complex optical conductivity σ(ω) of the sample can be obtained from the well-known Tinkham relation for a thin film [52]:

where n = 1.96 is the refractive index of sapphire substrate, Z 0 = 377 Ω is the impedance of free space, d is the thickness of CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure, σ(ω) = σ 1(ω) + iσ 2(ω) is the complex optical conductivity, and ω is the radiation frequency. For ML-hBN, dσ(ω) → σ(ω) being the optical conductivity because the thickness of ML-hBN is too thin so that it can be considered as a 2D electronic system. The real σ 1(ω) and imaginary σ 2(ω) parts of the conductivity for ML-hBN and for GCNDs/, BGCNDs/ and YCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures are shown as dots in Fig. 5(d)-(f) and in Fig. S3 (see Supplementary material) as a function of radiation frequency f = ω/2π at different temperatures. It can be found that σ 1(ω) and σ 2(ω) increase with temperature. The frequency dependences of THz transmittance for BGCNDs/ and YCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures are similar and, therefore, the dependence of their optical conductivities upon frequency is also similar. This is because ethanol and acetic acid solvents are easily volatile, and there are few chemical groups left in the CNDs during the formation of heterostructures. Thus, the THz optoelectronic properties of these two heterostructures are similar. Since DMF is not volatile in comparison to ethanol and acetic acid, more N- and O-based chemical bonds/functional groups remain in the CNDs during the solidification of CNDs. This makes the THz response of GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure differ significantly from BGCNDs/ and YCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures. It can be seen from Fig. 5(e) that σ 2(ω) of GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure is negative and gradually approaches to zero with increasing temperature. This means that the carriers in this heterostructure transfer the energy to the THz radiation field and their transmission ability gradually becomes weaker with increasing temperature.

The theoretical model of the optical conductivity induced by free carrier absorption can help us to understand the experimental results obtained from THz TDS measurement. It is known that the Drude model gives the simplest formula to describe the optical conductivity in an electron gas system. σ 1(ω) given by the Drude model decreases monotonically with increasing ω and σ 2(ω) should always be positive. The results shown in Fig. 5(e) for GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure obviously disobey the Drude formula. In 2001, Smith proposed a modified Drude model for optical conductivity of free electrons based on the impulse response method and the Poisson statistics. It is worth mentioning that the Drude-Smith model takes into account of the optically induced electronic backscattering or localization for free carriers. The Drude-Smith formula can be written as [53]:
(2)
σ
( ω )
=
σ 0
1 - i ω τ
1 +
c
1 - i ω τ
,
where σ 0 = ge 2 nτ/m* is the dc conductivity, n is the free carrier density, τ is the electronic relaxation time, m* is the effective mass of the carrier, g is the geometry factor associated with CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure, and c = [-1,0] represents the degree of the electronic backscattering or localization. In Eq. (2), only the first scattering term in the general Drude-Smith formula is taken into consideration. When c ∼ -1, the strongest effect of photon-induced carrier backscattering or localization can be achieved. When c ∼ 0, the Drude-Smith formula becomes the conventional Drude formula. In this study, we apply the Drude-Smith formula to fit the optical conductivity obtained experimentally. The results of fitting are shown as the solid curves in Fig. 5(d)-(f) and in Fig. S3 in Supplementary material. It can be seen that σ 1(ω) and σ 2(ω) given by the Drude-Smith formula fit very well with the corresponding experimental results for ML-hBN and for GCQDs/, BGCNDs/ and YCQDs/ML-hBN heterostructures. This indicates that the optical conductivity of CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure can be described rightly by the Drude-Smith model.

By fitting the real and imaginary parts of optical conductivity obtained experimentally with the Drude-Smith formula, we can determine the key sample parameters such as the correlation parameter of carrier density N = gn/m*, the electronic relaxation time τ, and the electronic localization factor c for ML-hBN and CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure. For ML-hBN alone, g = 1. Here we are not fully sure if CNDs/ML-hBN/sapphire samples are n- or p-type and what the geometry factor g is for CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures. Furthermore, the effective electron mass m* for free carrier in the heterostructure is also unknown. Therefore, we take N = gn/m* obtained experimentally to see the change of the carrier density with varying temperature on the basis that the effective electron mass and the geometry factor do not alter significantly with varying the temperature.

In Fig. 6 (a), the correlation parameters of carrier density N as a function of temperature are shown for different samples. It should be noted that ML-hBN is a 2D electronic material so that N = n/m* is with a dimension of cm−2·kg−1. In contrast, the thickness of CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure is on the order of microns and, therefore, the carrier density of the heterostructure is with a dimension of cm−3·kg−1. As expected, N increases with temperature T for all samples. We find that the temperature dependence of N for these samples can be described by the Arrhenius relation [47] (see solid curves in Fig. 6(a)):
(3)
N = A · exp
- Δ E /
k
B
T
,
where A is the fitting parameter, ΔE is the thermal activation energy, and kB is the Boltzmann constant. This indicates that CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure behaves like a semiconductor. Through fitting, we obtain the thermal activation energy ΔE = 4.3 meV for ML-hBN and 4.1 meV, 6.2 meV and 6.0 meV for GCNDs/, BGCNDs/ and YCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures respectively. These values of the activation energies are smaller than that of 10.77 meV for blue fluorescent CNDs [12]. This implies that the presence of the ML-hBN in the heterostructure can enhance the thermal excitation of free carriers into the heterostructure. Moreover, similar to hot electron injection from graphene quantum dots into, e.g., TiO2 [11], the formation of the heterostructure can also enhance the carrier injection and, thus, improve the transport capabilities of the sample device. From XPS results shown in Fig. 4, we learn that the N contents in GCNDs, BGCNDs and YCNDs are respectively 9.47%, 5.35% and 4.83%. Accordingly, N (ΔE) in GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure is higher (smaller) than those in BGCNDs/ and YCND/ML-hBN heterostructures. This suggests that the N-based functional groups and defects in CNDs play an important role in affecting the carrier density in the vdW heterostructure.

The temperature dependence of the electronic relaxation times τ in CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures are shown in Fig. 6(b) (see dots). τ decreases with increasing temperature for all samples, which is another typical feature of a semiconductor. Because CND layer is much thicker than ML-hBN, the scattering of carriers in the heterostructure occurs mainly in the CND layer. In CNDs, the scattering centers of carriers are mainly impurities, acoustic-phonons and longitudinal optic (LO)-phonons [54]. The influence of temperature on carrier interaction with these scattering centers can be approximately modeled as [54–56]:
(4)
1
τ
=
1
τ
I
+
α
AC
T
+
N
0
Γ
LO
,
where 1/τI , αAC and ΓLO are temperature-independent fitting parameters induced by carrier interaction with impurities, acoustic-phonons and LO-phonons respectively, N 0 = [exp(ELO /kBT)-1]-1 is the LO-phonon occupation number with ELO being the LO-phonon energy in CNDs. Considering that the C-cores of CNDs has the typical in-plane lattice of graphite crystal (see Fig. 2), the LO-phonon energy in CNDs can be taken as ELO = 198.8 meV for graphene quantum dots, which is determined by G-band Raman shift [57]. We apply Eq. (4) to fit the experimental results obtained for CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures (see curves in Fig. 6(b)). It is found that the good fitting can be achieved and the corresponding fitting parameters are shown in Table 2 . It should be noted that the charged impurities in CNDs come mainly from functional groups and defects attached to the surface/edges of the CNDs. The slow volatilization of DMF solvent during the preparation of GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure and the higher contents of C=O and N-based functional groups introduced by DMF solvent into the GCNDs make the GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure contain more charged impurities. Therefore, the carrier-impurity scattering in GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure is relatively stronger, that is, τI for GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure is shorter than those for BGCNDs/ and YCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures. The BGCNDs/ and YCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures are with similar solvent volatility and similar functional groups and chemical contents in CNDs. Thus, the strengths of their carrier-impurity scattering are similar. We find that similar to other carbon-based materials such as graphene [56] and diamond [58], the temperature dependence of τ in CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure is mainly induced by carrier coupling with acoustic-phonons in CND layer. The effect of LO-phonon scattering is relatively weak because the LO-phonon energy in CNDs is relatively large.

Very recently, we have found that when placing the nano-electronic materials, such as ML-MoS2 [59,60], ML-hBN [38,39], ML-WS2 [61], CNDs [12] etc., on dielectric substrate, the effect of photon-induced electronic backscattering or localization can be observed by using THz TDS. The electronic localization factor c as a function of temperature for ML-hBN and for three CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructures is shown in Fig. 6(c). We see that the c-factors for these samples decrease with increasing temperature (noting c is always a negative value), indicating that the effect of THz photon-induced localization of free carriers becomes weaker with increasing temperature. The formation of the heterostructures using BGCNDs and YCNDs can weaken this effect in comparison with ML-hBN. Again, because the types of chemical modifications on BGCNDs and YCNDs are quite similar, the carrier densities in these two samples are similar and the localization effects are also close. In contrast, GCNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure shows a strong effect of photon-induced localization. The reason is that more N- and O-based functional groups/bonds are present in GCNDs, which can play a role in constraining the carrier motion and, thus, can enhance the backscattering effect in the presence of THz radiation. Hence, different chemical modifications on CNDs can not only modulate the PL emission from CNDs (see Fig. 2(e)-(g)), but also affect the THz optoelectronic properties of CNDs/ML-hBN vdWHs.

From the fact that it is difficult to transfer carbon dots onto ML-hBN uniformly and stably by mechanical transfer, the solution infiltration method is used in this study to directly drop the solution with CNDs onto ML-hBN. This technique can be applied to promote the formation of the stable vdWH between CNDs and ML-hBN by low temperature annealing. In doing so, no obvious agglomeration of CNDs occurs on the surface of ML-hBN. Due to the vdW force between CNDs and ML-hBN, as well as to the vdW contacts among CNDs, the heterostructure can be formed very stably. The thickness of the formed CND layer depends on the concentration and dosage of CNDs solution dropped onto the M−hBN, as well as the temperature of the solvent evaporation. In order to fully ensure the reliability of the THz TDS measurement and to further control the uniformity of the vdWHs, the CND layer needs to completely cover the ML-hBN film. This can inevitably lead to a thicker CND layer. The thickness of CNDs/ML-hBN heterostructure can be considered during the analysis of the experimental data obtained from THz TDS measurement.

Similar to other vdWHs realized from layered 2D materials, CNDs/ML-hBN vdWHs can be considered as two resistors along the growth direction, resulting in an increase in its dc resistance. On the contrary, along the parallel direction, the heterostructure is equivalent to the parallel connection of two resistors, resulting in a decrease in its resistance. In our THz TDS measurement, the THz beam is transmitted vertically through the heterostructures. The polarization direction of THz electromagnetic field is parallel to the sample surface or 2D plane of ML-hBN. Thus, the THz response of carriers in the heterostructure is mainly achieved in the CND layer. Consequently, the conductance of CNDs/ML-hBN vdWHs (greater than 6 × 10-4 S) is higher than that of ML-hBN. The thicker CND layer can make heterostructure more like the graphite.

In this work, we have synthesized the CNDs with different chemical modifications. These CNDs have been applied to fabricate the CNDs/ML-hBN/sapphire based vdWHs for the first time. The basic physical properties of these vdWHs have been studied and examined by using THz TDS techniques. The main conclusions drawn from this study can be summarized as follows. i) From our XPS results, it can be inferred that different chemical solvents can introduce different chemical modifications, such as chemical bonds/functional groups, on the surface/edge of the C-cores of CNDs. ii) ML-hBN can be compatible with both CNDs and sapphire. The presence of ML-hBN in the heterostructure can greatly suppress the agglomeration of dried CNDs on the substrate. This technique can be applied to fabricate the practical CND-based electronic and optoelectronic devices. iii) It has been found that chemical modification on CNDs can affect strongly the electronic and optoelectronic properties of CNDs/ML-hBN vdWHs. In particular, GCNDs/ML-hBN vdWH with higher contents of N-based functional groups and C=O bonds on the surface/edge of CNDs can respond more strongly to THz irradiation. This heterostructure can have higher carrier density, shorter electronic relaxation time and stronger effect of photon-induced electronic localization. iv) By examining the temperature dependence of the carrier density and electronic relaxation time, we have found that the features of carrier transport in CNDs/ML-hBN vdWH are very much semiconductor-like. In short, the agglomeration of dried CNDs on the substrate can be greatly suppressed by placing ML-hBN in the heterostructure, while the electronic and optoelectronic properties of the heterostructure can be efficiently tunned and modified via chemical modification on CNDs. Therefore, both the presence of ML-hBN in the heterostructure and chemical modification on CNDs are important for a working device. One is important for sample fabrication and another one is important for controlling and modifying the physical properties of the heterostructure. We hope that this work can contribute to an in-depth understanding of the electronic and optoelectronic properties of CNDs and C-based vdWHs and provide a scientific basis for exploring of potential applications of related nanomaterials and devices.
